resources.string:
Definitions and Terminology of Functions: 
 • Constants vs. Variables: constants do not change, variables do. 
 • Function: a rule assigning each x in domain E to a unique y. 
 • Independent variable (x) and dependent variable (y); domain and range. 
 • Notation: y=f(x) or y(x) expresses dependency of y on x. 
 • Representations: analytical (formulas), tabular (tables), graphical (coordinate plane).

Methods and Properties of Functions: 
 • Analytical method: formulas define function values explicitly. 
 • Tabular method: list argument values and corresponding function values. 
 • Graphical method: plot points M(x,y) to find function values from the graph. 
 • Domain: all permissible x values; Range: all attainable y values. 
 • Practical uses: formulas for calculation, tables for observation, graphs for visualization.

Even, Odd, and Monotone Functions: 
 • Even function: f(-x)=f(x), symmetric about y-axis. 
 • Odd function: f(-x)=-f(x), symmetric about origin. 
 • Many functions are neither even nor odd. 
 • Monotone: increasing (x₁<x₂ ⇒ f(x₁)<f(x₂)), decreasing (x₁<x₂ ⇒ f(x₁)>f(x₂)). 
 • Intervals of constant sign and roots: where f(x) keeps sign or f(x)=0 (x-axis intersections).

Properties and Graphical Interpretation: 
 • Graph of even function mirrors across y-axis; odd function across origin. 
 • Increasing function rises; decreasing function falls on interval. 
 • Monotone functions maintain order: either always rising or falling. 
 • Intervals of constant sign: function remains positive or negative. 
 • Roots (zeros) are points where graph intersects x-axis.

Inverse Functions: Definition and Existence 
 • y=f(x) has an inverse if each y₀ in range corresponds to a unique x₀ in domain. 
 • Inverse function: x=g(y), defined on original range E, called inverse of y=f(x). 
 • Monotone functions (increasing or decreasing) guarantee existence of inverse. 
 • If f(x) increasing, g(y) increasing; if f(x) decreasing, g(y) decreasing. 
 • Find inverse algebraically: solve for x in terms of y; notation y=g(x).

Properties and Graphical Interpretation: 
 • Graphs of f(x) and g(x) symmetric about line y=x. 
 • Point M(x₀,y₀) on f(x) corresponds to N(y₀,x₀) on g(x). 
 • Inverse preserves order: increasing ↔ increasing, decreasing ↔ decreasing. 
 • Symmetry used to visualize and verify inverses graphically. 
 • Practical use: compute inverse values and check graphically.

Basic Functions: Linear, Inverse, Quadratic 
 • Linear: y=kx+b, graph is a straight line; k>0 increasing, k<0 decreasing. 
 • Inverse Proportionality: y=k/x, graph is a hyperbola, odd function symmetric about origin. 
 • Quadratic: y=ax²+bx+c, graph is parabola; vertex x₀=-b/2a, symmetry line x=x₀. 
 • Power Function: y=xⁿ, even n symmetric about y-axis, odd n symmetric about origin. 
 • Logarithmic Function: y=logₐx, defined x>0, inverse of exponential y=aˣ, increasing if a>1.

Function Graphs and Modulus 
 • Graphs of modulus functions: y=|x|, y=|2x+1|, y=x|x|, piecewise construction. 
 • y=|f(x)| obtained by reflecting negative part of y=f(x) about x-axis. 
 • Linear and quadratic functions: increasing/decreasing behavior determined by coefficients. 
 • Inverse proportionality: branches in first/third or second/fourth quadrants depending on sign of k. 
 • Quadratic parabola: branches up if a>0, down if a<0; vertex is max/min point.
